title: Star Wars radio dramas
content:
{{Top|rwm|reb|title=''Star Wars'' radio dramas}} {{Youmay|the radio drama trilogy|[[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'' (radio)]]}} {{Quote|A long time ago, in a galaxy far, far away... there came a time of revolution, when rebels united to challenge a tyrannical empire.|audio=ATimeOfRevolution.flac|[[Ken Hiller]]'s introduction to each radio episode|[[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'' radio drama]]}} {{Audiobook |image=[[File:Complete_SWTrilogy_NPR_CD.jpg]] |title=''Star Wars'' radio dramas |author=[[Brian Daley]] |narrator=[[Ken Hiller]] |producer=*[[Richard Toscan]]

{{C|''The Empire Strikes Back''}} *[[October 19]]–[[November 23]], [[1996]]
{{C|''Return of the Jedi''}} |rereleased=*[[March 24]], [[1993]] {{C|''Star Wars'' cassette and compact disc}}{{HighBridge|url=starwars.html|text=''Star Wars''}} *[[July 16]], [[1993]] {{C|''The Empire Strikes Back'' cassette and compact disc}}{{HighBridge|url=empirestrikesback.html|text=The Empire Strikes Back}} *[[October 30]], [[2007]] {{C|[[The Complete Star Wars Trilogy: The Original Radio Dramas|Boxed set]]}}{{HighBridge|url=starwarsthecompletetrilogy.html|text=Star Wars: The Complete Trilogy}} *[[November 6]], [[2013]] {{C|Collector's Edition}} |media type=*Radio broadcast *Cassette *Compact disc |run time=29 episodes, approximately 30 minutes each |discs= |cassettes= |pages= |isbn=9781622313242 |timeline=[[0 BBY/Legends|0 BBY]][[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'' radio drama]]–[[4 ABY/Legends|4 ABY]][[Return of the Jedi (radio)|''Return of the Jedi'' radio drama]] |series= |preceded by= |followed by= }} The '''''Star Wars'' radio dramas''' are expanded [[List of Legends audio productions|audio]] adaptations of the [[Original trilogy|original ''Star Wars'' trilogy]] first broadcast on [[National Public Radio]] in [[1981]], [[1983]], and [[1996]]. The first two radio series, [[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'']] and [[The Empire Strikes Back (radio)|''The Empire Strikes Back'']], were produced by NPR and broadcast on the program [[Wikipedia:NPR Playhouse|''NPR Playhouse'']]. The third, [[Return of the Jedi (radio)|''Return of the Jedi'']], was produced by [[HighBridge Audio]] by many of the same cast and production team. It was broadcast on NPR's ''[[wikipedia:L.A. Theatre Works|L.A. Theatre Works]]''. The radio [[Wikipedia:|serials]] were made with the full co-operation of [[George Lucas]], who for one dollar sold the rights to the [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|first ''Star Wars'' film]] to KUSC-FM, the public radio affiliate at his [[Wikipedia:Alma mater|alma mater]], the [[Wikipedia:University of Southern California|University of Southern California]]. The deal allowed the production team full use of the films' sound effects and musical score. Lucas made similar agreements for the other two films, selling the radio rights for one dollar each. Recordings of all three dramatizations were produced by HighBridge, and [[Del Rey]] published the three scripts in book form. Sw-radio.jpg|'''''[[Star Wars (radio)|Star Wars]]'''''
Also released as ''Star Wars: A New Hope'' Esb-radio.png|'''''[[The Empire Strikes Back (radio)|The Empire Strikes Back]]''''' Rotj radiodrama.jpg|''and '''[[Return of the Jedi (radio)|Return of the Jedi]]''''' ==Development== ===''Star Wars''=== {{Main|Star Wars (radio)}}
'''''Star Wars''''' is a 13-part, 6½-hour radio serial based on {{Film|IV}}. It was first broadcast on [[National Public Radio]] in [[1981]]. It was the brainchild of [[Richard Toscan]], who presented the idea to [[Lucasfilm]] and obtained the rights to ''Star Wars'' for KUSC, n NPR affiliate. Lucasfilm commissioned [[Brian Daley]] to write the script,''[[Star Wars: The National Public Radio Dramatization]]'' with Toscan assisting in the process to make sure the script was well adapted for radio.{{WebCite|url=https://www.orartswatch.org/interview-richard-toscan-champion-of-contemporary-audio-theater/ |text=Richard Toscan, champion of contemporary audio theater |author=Brett Campbell |work= Oregon ArtsWatch | date=2022-01-13 |archivedate=20220705213936 }} Daley's script fleshed out the film's storyline into thirteen half-hour episodes. The first two-and-a-half episodes provided backstory, some of which was taken from [[Content cut from Star Wars|deleted scenes]] from the film. One episode depicts [[Princess/Legends|Princess]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia]]'s first encounter with [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]];"[[Points of Origin]]" another shows how her ship obtains the [[Death Star plans/Legends|Death Star plans]] after the [[Battle of Toprawa]]."[[Black Knight, White Princess, and Pawns]]" [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker's]] first episode depicts his life on Tatooine, including a [[Skyhopper/Legends|skyhopper]] race in [[Beggar's Canyon/Legends|Beggar's Canyon]]—something alluded to in the film but never shown—and later his witnessing of the movie's opening battle through his [[Macrobinoculars/Legends|macrobinoculars]], which comes from a deleted scene."[[A Wind to Shake the Stars]]" Later additional scenes depict [[C-3PO/Legends|Threepio]] and [[R2-D2/Legends|Artoo's]] efforts to evade capture in the streets of [[Mos Eisley/Legends|Mos Eisley]],"[[The Han Solo Solution]]" Luke's [[Lightsaber combat/Legends|lightsaber combat]] training aboard ship,"[[Rogues, Rebels and Robots]]" and Leia's brutal psychological [[Torture/Legends|torture]] at Vader's hands, among other additions."[[Death Star's Transit]]" During production, [[John Madden]] directed the voice actors, and [[Tom Voegeli]] led the sound mixing in post-production, combining the recorded performance with the original film's music by [[John Williams]] and sound effects by [[Ben Burtt]]. NPR went to considerable effort to promote the drama, commissioning a poster, hosting an opening gala at Griffith Observatory in Los Angeles, and producing a making-of documentary, ''[[The Making of Star Wars For Radio: A Fable For the Mind's Eye|A Fable for the Mind's Eye]]''.''[[The Making of Star Wars For Radio: A Fable For the Mind's Eye|A Fable for the Mind's Eye]]'' Many of the actors involved in the movie were unavailable to reprise their roles: [[Harrison Ford]], for instance, was committed to the first [[Indiana Jones]] movie. Two of the actors, [[Mark Hamill]] and [[Anthony Daniels]], returned to reprise their roles as [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] and [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]] respectively.
The episodes were entitled: # "[[A Wind to Shake the Stars]]" # "[[Points of Origin]] # "[[Black Knight, White Princess, and Pawns]]" # "[[While Giants Mark Time]]" # "[[Jedi that Was, Jedi to Be]]" # "[[The Millennium Falcon Deal]]" # "[[The Han Solo Solution]]" # "[[Death Star's Transit]]" # "[[Rogues, Rebels and Robots]]" # "[[The Luke Skywalker Initiative]]" # "[[The Jedi Nexus]]" # "[[The Case for Rebellion]]" # "[[Force and Counterforce]]"

===''The Empire Strikes Back''=== {{Main|The Empire Strikes Back (radio)}}
The success of the first series led to a 10-part, five-hour series based on {{Film|V}}, which debuted on NPR on February 14, 1983. The three principal production leaders, Daley, Madden, and Voegli, returned, with Voegli getting an additional credit as co-director. All the principal cast members also reprised their roles. As with the first ''Star Wars'' radio drama, NPR launched an extensive promotional campaign.''[[The Empire Strikes Back: The National Public Radio Dramatization]]'' In one unusual promotion, [[Craig Claiborne]] was tapped to create a version of [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda's]] [[Rootleaf stew/Legends|rootleaf stew]] recipe, which the [[Jedi Master/Legends|Jedi Master]] served Luke in [[Yoda's Hut/Legends|his hut]] on [[Dagobah/Legends|Dagobah]]. The recipe ran in magazines and newspapers across the country.{{WebCite|url=https://starwarslivinghistory.com/2017/11/20/yoda-stew/ |text=Recipe: Yoda Stew |work= Living History in the Star Wars Galaxy: Applying the methods of historic reenacting to the Galaxy Far, Far Away |date= 2017-11-20 |archivedate=20240123010023 }} Like ''Star Wars,'' ''The Empire Strikes Back'' expanded on the film's story, incorporating new scenes to flesh out characters and events, though its shorter ten-episode run meant that such additions could not be as long. The first episode begins before the film's opening scene and features an Imperial attack on a Rebel patrol in the [[Battle of Derra IV]], followed by depictions of the Rebels' work to establish [[Echo Base/Legends|their hidden base]]."[[Freedom's Winter]]" A later scene shows [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo's]] efforts to keep [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] alive while stranded overnight in an [[emergency shelter]] in the frozen wastelands of [[Hoth/Legends|Hoth]]."[[A Question of Survival]]" Still later, the cyborg [[Lobot/Legends|Lobot]], mute for the entirety of the film, is given a few terse lines of dialogue."[[Dark Lord's Fury]]" [[Billy Dee Williams]] reprised [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]], and [[John Lithgow]] played [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]]; at the time Madden was directing Lithgow in the play ''[[wikipedia:Beyond Therapy|Beyond Therapy]]''.
The episodes were titled: # "[[Freedom's Winter]]" # "[[The Coming Storm]]" # "[[A Question of Survival]]" # "[[Fire and Ice]]" # "[[The Millennium Falcon Pursuit]]" # "[[Way of the Jedi (episode)|Way of the Jedi]]" # "[[New Allies, New Enemies]]" # "[[Dark Lord's Fury]]" # "[[Gambler's Choice]]" # "[[The Clash of Lightsabers]]"

===''Return of the Jedi''=== {{Main|Return of the Jedi (radio)}}
NPR's plans for a third radio serial based on {{Film|VI}} were put on hold when federal funding for public radio was dramatically reduced during the administration of [[Wikipedia:Ronald Reagan|Ronald Reagan]]. In [[1996]] [[HighBridge Audio]], the company that had released the first two series on tape and CD, finally undertook the production of the third installment of the trilogy. ''Return of the Jedi'' was written in six episodes for a running time of about three hours. The core production team of Daley, Madden and Voegli reassembled, as did most of the principal cast members, though notably not Mark Hamill or Billy Dee Williams, whose roles had to be recast. Scriptwriter Brian Daley was ill with cancer during production and in fact died on the day that recording concluded; therefore [[John Whitman]] was hired to handle necessary rewrites and additions that would be identified by the director and cast. However, in most cases Madden accepted Daley's script without changes, and Whitman is credited as an additional writer only for the first two episodes.[[Return of the Jedi: The National Public Radio Dramatization|''Return of the Jedi'': The National Public Radio Dramatization]] Despite its much shorter running time than the two preceding series, ''Return of the Jedi'' followed them in adding additional scenes and dialogue to the story. Many of these drew connections to the growing [[Star Wars Legends|''Star Wars'' Expanded Universe]], making references to events from the [[Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy|Thrawn Trilogy]], ''[[Dark Empire]]'', and ''[[Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire (novel)|Shadows of the Empire]],'' the last of which was published a few months before the radio drama's premier. The opening scene, depicting Luke Skywalker's construction of [[Luke Skywalker's green lightsaber/Legends|his new lightsaber]], follows directly on the epilogue of ''Shadows of the Empire.''''[[Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire (novel)|Shadows of the Empire]]'' Another scene depicts a conversation between C-3PO and Thrawn Trilogy character [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]], who is undercover in the palace of [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]]."[[Tatooine Haunts]]" The adaptation used many of the original radio cast, though [[Joshua Fardon]] took over as Luke and [[Arye Gross]] replaced Billy Dee Williams as Lando. [[Ed Begley, Jr.]] was the voice of [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]] and [[Ed Asner]], speaking only in grunts, guest-stars as [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]]. The only actor who starred in both the feature films and all three radio dramas was [[Anthony Daniels]].
The episodes were titled: # "[[Tatooine Haunts]]" # "[[Fast Friends]]" # "[[Prophecies and Destinies]]" # "[[Pattern and Web]]" # "[[So Turns a Galaxy, So Turns a Wheel]]" # "[[Blood of a Jedi]]"

===Other broadcasts and releases===
*There are several radio promos, deleted scenes, and additional music tracks available that originated on previous releases of this collection and in the NPR broadcast versions. *[[Wikipedia:BBC Radio 1|BBC Radio 1]] broadcast the series in the UK, with a key scene in the final episode clumsily cut for timing. *All three series have been released on cassette tape and CD.
The tracks are: # "Radio Promo #1 - Anthony Daniels" # "Radio Promo #2 - Ann Sachs" # "Radio Promo #3 - Mark Hamill" # "Star Wars Radio Drama - Additional Music" # "Star Wars Radio Drama - Alternate Take 'Your Father's Lightsaber'" # "Star Wars Radio Drama - Alternate Take 'Bail and Leia'" # "Return Of The Jedi Radio Drama - Alternate Take 'Speederbike Chase'" # "The Making Of The Radio Dramas"

==Continuity== The earliest statements about [[Canon|''Star Wars'' canon]], made in [[1994]] in ''[[A Guide to the Star Wars Universe, Second Edition, Revised and Expanded|A Guide to the Star Wars Universe]]'' and [[Star Wars Insider 23|''Star Wars Insider'' 23]], gave a privileged status to the radio dramas and [[The Star Wars Trilogy|novelizations]] alongside the films, considering them all to be official benchmarks for what was in the ''Star Wars'' universe.''[[A Guide to the Star Wars Universe, Second Edition, Revised and Expanded]]''[[Star Wars Insider 23|''Star Wars Insider'' 23]] In [[2000]], Lucasfilm reorganized the canon and specified that the highest level, "G-canon," was reserved the films only.{{Blog|holocron|33|What'd I say again? Select posts by me regarding continuity}}{{SWArchive|url=community/askjc/steve/askjc20000424.html|text=Ask the Lucasfilm Jedi Council - April 24, 2000}} Since then, anything in the radio dramas beyond the events of the films has been part of the Expanded Universe, which became [[Star Wars Legends]] in 2014. ==Media== ===Editions=== *{{ISBN|9781622313242}}; [[November 6]], [[2013]]; [[HighBridge Audio]]; Collector's Edition: Light Side *{{ISBN|9781622313259}}; November 6, 2013; HighBridge Audio; Collector's Edition: Dark Side{{HighBridge|url=starwarsdarkside.html|text=''Star Wars: A New Hope
